summary: Jeff Atwood explains Markov chains through the lens of Garkov, a project that generates new Garfield comic dialogue using Markov text synthesis. He walks through how Markov chains work by showing progressively higher-order text generation examples from Programming Pearls, demonstrating how each letter or word is chosen based on statistical probability from a training corpus. The post then pivots to show the surprising practical applications of this simple technique: Google's PageRank algorithm is fundamentally a Markov chain modeling random web surfers, and hidden Markov models power advanced spam filtering. Atwood marvels at how such a simple statistical method—with no memory of previous states—can produce remarkably coherent output given sufficient training data.
key_points: Markov chains generate text by randomly selecting each word based on statistical probability of what historically follows the current word(s) in a training corpus
The technique is remarkably simple—no memory of previous states, just a lookup table and dice roll—yet produces surprisingly coherent output
Google's PageRank algorithm is fundamentally a Markov chain where states are pages and transitions are links between them
Hidden Markov models extend this concept to power spam filtering systems like CRM114 that outperform basic Bayesian filters
The quality of Markov output depends entirely on the input corpus, making the vast web and email archives ideal training data
money_quotes: What's amazing to me about Markov chains is how unbelievably simple they are.

A Markov chain has no memory of previous states: the next state is chosen based on a random dice roll and a lookup into a table of the states that tend to historically follow the current state in the input corpus.
Given an adequate input corpus, they work almost uncannily well, a testament to the broad power of rudimentary statistical inference.
PageRank can be understood as a Markov chain in which the states are pages, and the transitions are all equally probable and are the links between pages.
But Markov chains aren't just useful for automatically generating Paul Graham essay parodies. They're also quite practical.
If you play with the Markov text synthesizer, you'll quickly find that Markov methods are only as good as their input corpus.
But it's sure tough to imagine a more ideal input corpus for Markovian techniques than the unimaginable vastness of web and email, isn't it?
key_insight: Markov chains demonstrate how remarkably powerful simple statistical methods can be—the same basic technique that generates absurdist Garfield parodies also underlies Google's PageRank and advanced spam filtering.
